10-robotics/algorithms/Spatial-Algebra-a.title: Spatial algebra and the Articulated-Body Algorithm
10-robotics/algorithms/Spatial-Algebra-a.content:
Summary
Forward dynamics on a kinematic tree — given (q, \dot q, \tau, f^{ext}), solve for \ddot q — can be done in O(n) without ever forming the joint-space inertia M(q). The Articulated-Body Algorithm (ABA) propagates a 6×6 articulated inertia I^A_i and a 6-vector bias p^A_i through the tree such that the inboard force satisfies f_i = I^A_i a_i + p^A_i, then unfolds joint accelerations in a third sweep. The construction rests on (i) Plücker spatial algebra (c.f. Pluecker-coordinates) with its motion/force duality, (ii) the body-frame EOM I\dot\nu + \nu \times^* I\nu = \phi^{ext}, and (iii) an induction over the tree that eliminates each \ddot q_c via the joint constraint \tau_c = S_c^\top f_c at the joint frame.
Core Mechanism
Spatial algebra primitives. A twist \nu = (\omega, v_O) \in \mathbb{R}^6 lives in motion space M^6; a wrench \phi = (n_O, f) \in \mathbb{R}^6 lives in dual force space F^6. Pairing \langle \phi, \nu \rangle = n_O^\top \omega + f^\top v_O is frame-invariant. Cross operators:
\nu\times = \begin{bmatrix} \hat\omega & 0 \\ \hat v_O & \hat\omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nu\times^* = \begin{bmatrix} \hat\omega & \hat v_O \\ 0 & \hat\omega \end{bmatrix}, \quad (\nu\times)^\top = -\nu\times^*.
Motion transforms X^{B,A} act on twists; force vectors use the dual X^{B,A*} = (X^{A,B})^\top.
Body-frame EOM. Newton’s law \dot h^0 = \phi^{ext,0} in the inertial frame pulls back via the structural identity
\dot X^{0,B} = X^{0,B}(\nu^B \times),
which is the 6×6 adjoint analogue of R^\top \dot R = \hat\omega on SO(3). Transposing and dualizing yields \dot X^{0,B*} = X^{0,B*}(\nu^B \times^*), hence the transport theorem for co-vectors:
\frac{d^0 h^0}{dt} = X^{0,B*}\Big( \frac{d^B h^B}{dt} + \nu^B \times^* h^B \Big).
Substituting h = I\nu with I constant in body coords gives
I\dot\nu + \nu \times^* I\nu = \phi^{ext}. \tag{NE}
The \nu\times^*I\nu term is not part of momentum — it is the correction for differentiating a co-vector in a frame that moves with the body. Restricting to pure rotation about the CoM (\nu = (\omega,0), I = \text{diag}(I_c, mI_3)) recovers I_c\dot\omega + \omega \times I_c \omega = \tau, the classical Euler equation.
Kinematic recurrence (Pass 1). For body i with parent \lambda(i):
\nu_i = X^{i,\lambda(i)}\nu_{\lambda(i)} + S_i \dot q_i, \quad a_i = X^{i,\lambda(i)} a_{\lambda(i)} + S_i \ddot q_i + \underbrace{\nu_i \times S_i \dot q_i}_{=: c_i}, \quad p_i = \nu_i \times^* I_i \nu_i - f^{ext}_i.
Body i‘s spatial Newton-Euler in body-i coords:
f_i - \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} X^{i,c*} f_c = I_i a_i + p_i. \tag{NE-tree}
Articulated-body relation, proved by induction leaves → root. Claim:
f_i = I^A_i a_i + p^A_i. \tag{$\star$}
Base case (leaf). No children, so (NE-tree) gives f_\ell = I_\ell a_\ell + p_\ell, hence I^A_\ell = I_\ell, p^A_\ell = p_\ell.
Inductive step. Assume (\star) for each child c. From Pass 1, a_c = X^{c,i} a_i + S_c \ddot q_c + c_c =: a'_c + S_c \ddot q_c. Substitute into f_c = I^A_c a_c + p^A_c:
f_c = I^A_c a'_c + U_c \ddot q_c + p^A_c, \quad U_c := I^A_c S_c.
Apply joint constraint \tau_c = S_c^\top f_c and use symmetry of I^A_c (so S_c^\top I^A_c = U_c^\top):
\ddot q_c = D_c^{-1}\big(u_c - U_c^\top a'_c\big), \quad D_c := S_c^\top U_c, \quad u_c := \tau_c - S_c^\top p^A_c.
Back-substitute to eliminate \ddot q_c from f_c:
f_c = \tilde I^A_c, a'_c + U_c D_c^{-1} u_c + p^A_c, \quad \tilde I^A_c := I^A_c - U_c D_c^{-1} U_c^\top.
The -U_c D_c^{-1} U_c^\top term projects the free joint axis S_c out of I^A_c: on the direction the joint accommodates, no inertia is felt. Sum the children into (NE-tree) and identify with (\star) to read off the recurrences:
I^A_i = I_i + \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} X^{i,c*}, \tilde I^A_c, X^{c,i} \tag{R.1}
p^A_i = p_i + \sum_{c \in \text{ch}(i)} X^{i,c*}\Big( \tilde I^A_c, c_c + U_c D_c^{-1} u_c + p^A_c \Big) \tag{R.2}
Induction closes. ∎
The three passes.

























PassDirectionComputes1root → leaves\nu_i, c_i, p_i; init I^A_i \leftarrow I_i,, p^A_i \leftarrow p_i2leaves → rootU_i, D_i, u_i, \tilde I^A_i; accumulate I^A_{\lambda(i)}, p^A_{\lambda(i)} via (R.1, R.2)3root → leavesa'_i = X^{i,\lambda(i)} a_{\lambda(i)} + c_i; \ddot q_i = D_i^{-1}(u_i - U_i^\top a'_i); a_i = a'_i + S_i \ddot q_i
Gravity is folded in by setting a_0 = -g. Each pass touches every link with fixed-size 6×6 operations: O(n) total.
CRBA cousin. (R.1) mirrors the CRBA recurrence I^C_i = I_i + \sum_c X^{i,c*} I^C_c X^{c,i}. The only structural change is I^C_c \to \tilde I^A_c: CRBA locks every joint (children contribute full composite inertias), ABA frees them (the joint axis is projected out). One substitution distinguishes lock-everything inverse dynamics from let-everything-move forward dynamics.
RNEA duality. The inverse-dynamics relation that drops out is \tau_i = D_i \ddot q_i + U_i^\top a'_i + S_i^\top p^A_i. RNEA computes this given \ddot q; ABA inverts it for \ddot q given \tau.
Why It Matters
ABA is what makes real-time forward dynamics on a humanoid tractable. For a Unitree G1 (n \approx 23 joints), CRBA-plus-Cholesky is dominated by an O(n^3) linear solve — order 10^4 flops just for the inversion — while ABA is O(n) with no global matrix assembly, on the order of 10^2 per-link 6×6 operations. This is the kernel that lets a Whole-Body-MPC stack integrate a 23-DOF model at 1 kHz, that lets PPO/SAC training rollouts hit hundreds of thousands of env-steps per second on CPU, and that makes Pinocchio’s aba(model, data, q, v, tau) a hot inner loop rather than a bottleneck. The crossover where ABA beats CRBA-plus-Cholesky is n \gtrsim 9, well below any humanoid.
Pinocchio’s analytic derivatives — needed for DDP/iLQR-style trajectory optimization and for backprop through dynamics in differentiable simulation — differentiate through the three passes, which is only tractable because each pass is a local recurrence over fixed-size blocks. There is no M^{-1} Jacobian to invert.
The body-frame EOM derivation matters specifically because \nu \times^* I \nu is the most commonly bungled piece of multibody dynamics code. It is not a Lagrangian Coriolis term, it is a coordinate-transport correction; once you see it as the dual of R^\top \dot R = \hat\omega, the spatial form writes itself instead of degenerating into a stacked 3+3 mess that has to be re-derived every time the frame convention changes.
Limitations

Tree topology only. Closed loops (parallel five-bar legs, four-bar linkages, dual-arm grasping, a humanoid with both feet planted treated rigidly) break the parent-map recurrence. The standard fix — cut loops, run ABA on the spanning tree, project constraint forces via Lagrange multipliers — re-introduces an O(m^3) solve over m loop-closure constraints, eroding the O(n) win.
Floating-base inversion cost. For a 6-DOF floating base, D_0 is 6×6 and dominates the per-step constant factor. Most of the wall-clock budget on a humanoid sits in this single block, not in the joint chain.
Symmetry is exact-arithmetic-only. I^A_i is symmetric by induction (R.1 is a congruence sandwich, UD^{-1}U^\top is symmetric), but FP rounding breaks it across hundreds of recurrence updates. Production code symmetrizes explicitly each iteration.
No contact dynamics for free. ABA produces \ddot q for prescribed \tau and external wrenches at the link frames. Rigid unilateral contact (LCP with \lambda \geq 0, \phi \geq 0) sits outside ABA and is typically solved via a Delassus-matrix construction that calls ABA n_c times for n_c contact points.
Rigid bodies, joint actuators only. Reflected rotor inertia is a one-line patch (add \gamma_i^2 I_{\text{rotor},i} to D_i). Link flexibility, gear backlash, and series-elastic actuation are not.

Open Questions

Reverse-mode AD through ABA. Forward-mode AD is straightforward (push tangents through the three passes). Reverse mode requires reversing the topological order and the cross-pass data dependencies (Pass 3 reads I^A_i, U_i, D_i produced in Pass 2 from \nu_i, p_i produced in Pass 1). What’s the minimal checkpointing scheme, and how does AD-generated code for \partial \ddot q / \partial q, \partial \ddot q / \partial \tau compare to Pinocchio’s hand-derived analytic Jacobians on cost and accuracy?
Residual-learning injection point. Plausible hooks: per-link bias on p^A_i (additive correction, preserves O(n) structure and locality), per-joint multiplier on D_i^{-1} (multiplicative gain on response, also local but breaks linearity in \tau), or a whole-tree post-hoc \Delta \ddot q correction (loses interpretability and any structural prior). The per-link bias seems strictly dominant — worth verifying on a real sim-to-real gap dataset.
Centroidal-momentum fusion. Wensing & Orin (2016) compute A_G(q) in O(n) using the same articulated-inertia scaffolding. Is there a single fused sweep producing both \ddot q and A_G \dot q, or does the algebra force two distinct passes?
Floating-base D_0 structure. In the centroidal frame the 6×6 base inertia decouples into angular and linear blocks. Worth checking whether exploiting this drops the base-solve below its current ~200-flop cost on a humanoid.
Implementation sanity. Code ABA from scratch on a 3-link planar chain and verify \ddot q against M^{-1}(\tau - h) computed via CRBA + Cholesky (Exercise 4 of the deck). Worth doing once with naked spatial algebra, no Pinocchio, to internalize the recurrences.

Links
CRBA-composite-rigid-body-algorithm RNEA-recursive-newton-euler Pluecker-coordinates spatial-inertia centroidal-momentum-matrix Pinocchio-internals forward-dynamics contact-dynamics-LCP differentiable-physics se3-adjoint

20-embedded/compilers/Register-Allocatio.title: Register Allocation and Spilling
20-embedded/compilers/Register-Allocatio.content:
Summary
After instruction selection, the compiler must map an unbounded set of SSA virtual registers to a fixed physical register file plus stack. The canonical formulation is graph k-coloring on the interference graph, which is NP-complete for k \geq 3, so production compilers use heuristic allocators (Chaitin-Briggs, linear scan, greedy, PBQP). When coloring fails, the allocator picks a vreg to spill to memory according to a cost heuristic and retries.

Core Mechanism
Setup. Liveness analysis produces a live range [s_v, e_v] for each vreg v. Two vregs interfere if their ranges overlap. The interference graph G = (V, E) has V = {\text{vregs}} and E = {(u,v) : \text{ranges overlap}}. With k physical registers, the allocation problem is:
\min_{S \subseteq V} \sum_{v \in S} c(v) \quad \text{s.t. } G[V \setminus S] \text{ is } k\text{-colorable}
where c(v) is the spill cost of v and S is the spill set. Both the feasibility check (k-colorability) and the objective are NP-hard, so all practical work approximates simultaneously.
Chaitin-Briggs (graph coloring). Iterate until colored:

Simplify: while \exists v with \deg(v) < k, push v onto stack, remove from G. The simplification lemma: a node with fewer than k neighbors is always colorable after its neighbors are.
Potential spill: if no such v exists, pick spill candidate v^* = \arg\min_v c(v), remove it.
Select: pop stack, greedily assign a color \notin colors of colored neighbors.
Rewrite: if any node was marked for spill, insert loads/stores at uses/defs, re-run liveness, restart.

Chaitin’s spill cost heuristic:
c(v) = \frac{(|\text{defs}(v)| + |\text{uses}(v)|) \cdot w_{\text{loop}}(v)}{\deg_G(v)}
High degree → spilling relieves more pressure; low use count → cheap to reload.
Linear scan. Sort live intervals by start point. Maintain active set A of currently-allocated intervals. For each new interval i: expire any j \in A with e_j < s_i (free its reg); if |A| < k assign a free reg; else spill the interval in A \cup {i} with latest end point. O(n \log n) vs. Chaitin’s O(n^2), at the cost of over-approximating interference (two intervals that overlap on the timeline may not actually interfere in the SSA dataflow sense).
Iterated Register Coalescing (George-Appel). Extends Chaitin-Briggs with conservative move elimination. Coalesce a, b across mov a, b iff one of:

George: every neighbor t of a with \deg(t) \geq k already interferes with b.
Briggs: the merged node ab has fewer than k neighbors of degree \geq k.

These tests guarantee coalescing never makes a colorable graph uncolorable.
PBQP (Partitioned Boolean Quadratic Problem). Formulates allocation as minimizing \sum_v C_v(x_v) + \sum_{(u,v) \in E} C_{uv}(x_u, x_v) where x_v is v‘s register assignment, C_v is a per-node cost vector (preferences, spill cost), and C_{uv} is an interference penalty matrix. Solved via belief propagation. Still NP-hard but tractable for realistic graph sizes.
Why It Matters
This is the pass that decides, for every variable you write, whether it lives in a register or thrashes through L1 — and it runs every time anything is compiled. Concretely:

JIT compilers (V8, HotSpot, LLVM -O0) use linear scan because compile time is on the critical path of execution; the spill penalty is acceptable.
AOT optimizing builds (-O2/-O3) use greedy or IRC because the user pays compile time once and runtime forever.
GPU compilers treat register allocation as an occupancy problem: a kernel using too many registers reduces the number of concurrent warps, so the spill/occupancy tradeoff is more important than spill count alone.
Embedded targets with k = 8 or fewer GPRs (AVR, MSP430) make spill decisions visible in benchmark numbers — a missed coalesce in a tight loop can double cycle count.

Limitations

All practical allocators are heuristic; for adversarial graphs they spill more than the optimum. The gap rarely matters but is unbounded in principle.
Spill cost models treat all memory accesses identically — they don’t model L1 vs. L2 reload, store-to-load forwarding, or the fact that a spilled vreg in a hot loop may be the actual bottleneck even with low “cost.”
Linear scan’s interval abstraction is strictly weaker than the interference graph: it can force spills that graph coloring would avoid. The damage is small on straight-line code, larger on irregular control flow.
Phase ordering: register allocation and instruction scheduling each constrain the other. Schedule-first inflates live ranges; allocate-first removes scheduling freedom. Most compilers pick one and live with it.
Aggressive coalescing without George/Briggs tests increases degree and causes spills (the original Chaitin bug).
SSA destruction (where the allocator leaves SSA form) introduces parallel copies that interact subtly with coalescing.

Open Questions

How much do learned spill-cost predictors (cf. MLGO-style approaches) actually move the needle on standard benchmarks vs. the Chaitin heuristic? The function looks crude but is hard to beat.
For GPU codegen, is there a principled way to fold occupancy targets into the allocator’s objective rather than running allocation under a hard register budget set by an outer loop?
PBQP is “real” optimization but rarely shipped — what’s the actual compile-time cost on a kernel-scale function, and where does it win over greedy in practice?
Live-range splitting (breaking a long-lived vreg into pieces, each separately allocatable) is where modern allocators get most of their wins over Chaitin-Briggs. Implementing it well is the actual engineering problem.

Links
ssa-form liveness-analysis graph-coloring instruction-scheduling llvm-codegen-pipeline pbqp

20-embedded/compilers/chaitin-register-a.title: Register Allocation & Spilling via Graph Coloring
20-embedded/compilers/chaitin-register-a.content:
Summary
Mapping unbounded symbolic registers to k physical registers is equivalent to k-coloring an interference graph; when k-coloring fails, nodes must be spilled to memory. Chaitin extends the coloring algorithm to make spill decisions inside the same graph-reduction loop, guided by a cost/degree heuristic. The result replaces ad hoc spill heuristics with a unified, principled algorithm that produces better code in less compile time.
Core Mechanism
Interference graph construction. Two symbolic registers r_i, r_j interfere iff one is live at a definition point of the other. Build G = (V, E) where V = symbolic registers, E = interference pairs. Represented as: symmetric |V| \times |V| bit matrix (O(1) adjacency query) + per-node adjacency vector (O(deg) sequential scan). Two-pass: first pass computes degrees; second fills adjacency vectors.
Coalescing (subsumption). For each copy instruction r_i \leftarrow r_j: if {r_i, r_j} \notin E, merge nodes, eliminating the copy. Iterate until fixpoint (2–3 passes in practice).
Coloring via simplification. Chaitin’s algorithm for k-coloring G:

If \exists node v with \deg(v) < k: remove v, recurse on G' = G \setminus {v}, reinsert v, assign any color not used by neighbors. Correctness: v has < k neighbors so a free color always exists.
If all nodes have \deg \geq k: algorithm is blocked — must spill.

Spill selection when blocked. Pick node v^* to spill:
v^* = \arg\min_{v \in V} \frac{\text{cost}(v)}{\deg(v)}
where
\text{cost}(v) = \sum_{\text{def/use sites } s \text{ of } v} \text{freq}(s)
with \text{freq}(s) \approx 10^{\text{loop_depth}(s)} (loop nesting approximation). Special cases: recomputable values and copy sources/targets can have cost 0 or negative. Local-only values with no intervening deaths get \text{cost} = \infty (spilling them cannot free a color).
Spill insertion and iteration. Spilling v replaces it with short-lived temporaries: a fresh r_{\text{tmp}} loaded immediately before each use, stored immediately after each definition. Rebuild G, reattempt coloring. Repeat until a k-coloring succeeds (converges rapidly; successive graphs shrink).
Full pipeline:
build_graph → coalesce → color_graph
    └─ blocked? → estimate_costs → decide_spills
                → insert_spill_code → rebuild_graph → retry

Why It Matters
This lets you generate near-hand-coded register schedules from a high-level language compiler in a target with many registers (e.g., 32 GPRs on the IBM 801, or modern RISC-V/ARM with 31 integer registers). Previously, spill decisions were made by ad hoc local heuristics after coloring failed, producing redundant load/store pairs and burning compile time. The graph-integrated approach amortizes the spill decision into the same O(|V| + |E|) simplification pass — the bottleneck is building the graph the first time; all retries operate on strictly smaller graphs.
Concrete scenario: a hot inner loop with 40 live values competing for 32 registers. The old approach might spill 8 values naively; Chaitin’s heuristic spills the ones cheapest to reload (e.g., loop-invariant constants that can be recomputed), minimizing added load/store traffic inside the loop body.
Limitations

Spill cost is approximate. Loop depth proxy (\times 10 per level) is a heuristic, not a profile. Mispredicts for irregular control flow (e.g., cold branches inside hot loops).
Graph rebuild after spill is not exact. Spilling a globally-live value should produce multiple short-lived nodes immediately; instead the graph is rebuilt from scratch, requiring iteration. In pathological cases this loop can require many passes.
NP-completeness evaded, not solved. The simplification heuristic is not guaranteed to find the minimum-spill solution; it is a greedy approximation. Optimal k-coloring is NP-complete.
Coalescing is conservative. Only non-interfering copies are coalesced; no attempt is made to break interferences to enable coalescing (cf. later work on iterated coalescing by George & Appel, 1996).
Memory pressure. The full |V|^2 bit matrix must fit in core during allocation — noted explicitly as a practical constraint.

Open Questions

How does the cost heuristic interact with profile-guided optimization? Replacing the loop-depth proxy with actual branch frequencies is straightforward in principle but requires PGO infrastructure.
What is the convergence bound on the spill-rebuild loop? The paper claims rapid convergence empirically but gives no formal bound.
George & Appel (1996) revisit coalescing — how much does conservative coalescing here leave on the table in copy-heavy IR (e.g., SSA with many φ-copies)?
Does the \text{cost}/\deg heuristic still perform well on architectures with register classes (FP vs. integer, vector), where the interference graph is partitioned?
Can the bit-matrix representation be replaced with a sparse structure (e.g., CSR) without losing the O(1) adjacency query, to relieve the memory constraint?

Links
graph-coloring liveness-analysis ssa-form compiler-intermediate-representation register-pressure loop-optimization
